subject: Civics
title: Growing up as Boys and Girls

questions.text: In the 1920s, in which islands did children not go to school and instead learned household tasks?
questions.explanation: In Samoa, children learned how to take care of siblings and do fishing from a very young age.

questions.text: At what age were Samoan boys allowed to go on fishing expeditions with older boys?
questions.explanation: After age nine, boys joined older boys to learn outdoor jobs like fishing and planting coconuts.

questions.text: Societies make clear distinctions between boys and girls from a ________ age.
questions.explanation: Distinctions are often made through the toys children are given, like dolls for girls and cars for boys.

questions.text: Which of these is a common stereotype about boys in many societies?
questions.explanation: Boys are often taught to be tough and that crying is a sign of weakness.

questions.text: In the 1960s in Madhya Pradesh, the girls' school was designed with a ________ in the middle.
questions.explanation: The courtyard provided a secluded and safe place where they could play in total privacy.

questions.text: Why did girls in 1960s Madhya Pradesh walk to school in groups?
options: For fear of being teased or attacked
questions.explanation: Walking in groups gave them a sense of security while using the public streets.

questions.text: Our society values the work done by men ________ than the work done by women.
questions.explanation: Domestic work done by women is often seen as 'natural' and is not recognized as real work.

questions.text: Housework is actually ________ and time-consuming.
questions.explanation: Housework involves heavy tasks like carrying water, firewood, and standing for long hours while cooking.

questions.text: What is the main reason why the work of housewives is 'invisible'?
options: It is done inside the home and not paid for
questions.explanation: Since it does not produce a direct income, housework is often taken for granted.

questions.text: Across the world, the main responsibility for housework and care-giving lies with:
questions.explanation: This includes looking after the family, especially children, the elderly, and sick members.

questions.text: Equality is an important principle of our ________.
questions.explanation: The Constitution says that being male or female should not be a reason for discrimination.

questions.text: To help women work outside, the government has passed laws to set up ________.
questions.explanation: Crèches or childcare centres allow women to seek employment and help girls attend school.

questions.text: The government has made it mandatory for organizations with more than ________ women employees to provide crèche facilities.
questions.explanation: This law ensures that working mothers have a safe place to keep their children during work hours.

questions.text: Harmeet's family in the story believed that Jaspreet (the mother) ________.
questions.explanation: They felt she stayed at home all day and hence didn't 'work', prompting her to go on strike.

questions.text: Many domestic workers are ________.
questions.explanation: Women form a large part of the domestic workforce in both rural and urban areas.

questions.text: The lives of domestic workers like Melani involve ________.
questions.explanation: They often start work as early as 5 AM and finish late at night with very little rest.

questions.text: The term 'Care-giving' includes looking after ________.
questions.explanation: It is a vital aspect of domestic work that is almost entirely handled by women.

questions.text: Gender inequality is a ________ issue, not just a personal one.
questions.explanation: It is rooted in the way society is organized and requires government action to fix.

questions.text: The toys given to children are a way of telling them they will have ________ futures.
questions.explanation: Toys signal the roles they are expected to play as adults.

questions.text: Valuing housework means ________.
questions.explanation: Societal attitudes need to change to appreciate the effort and time spent in domestic chores.

questions.text: Which of these is a physically demanding domestic task?
questions.explanation: In rural areas, women walk long distances to provide these basic necessities for their families.

questions.text: In the Samoan islands, girls continued to look after younger children until they were ________.
questions.explanation: Once they were teenagers, they had much more freedom and eventually learned weaving and cooking.

questions.text: The feeling of 'being a boy' or 'being a girl' is part of one's ________.
questions.explanation: Gender is a major part of how individuals see themselves and are seen by others.

questions.text: The 'double burden' of women's work refers to ________.
options: Doing two jobs at an office
Working both inside and outside the home
questions.explanation: Many women manage household chores as well as professional careers, effectively working two 'shifts'.

questions.text: Providing crèche facilities helps in ________.
questions.explanation: Older girls often stay home to mind younger siblings; crèches free them to attend school.

questions.text: In many societies, girls are taught to be ________.
questions.explanation: This is part of the gender socialization process that prepares them for specific social roles.

questions.text: Who does the cooking in Samoan society for special meals?
questions.explanation: In Samoa, boys did most of the cooking while girls helped with preparations.

questions.text: When we say housework is 'invisible', we mean it is ________.
questions.explanation: It is work that is done behind the scenes and rarely appreciated as a contribution to the economy.

questions.text: Most girls in the Madhya Pradesh story used the streets ________.
options: Simply as a place to get from point A to B
questions.explanation: Unlike boys who used streets for playing or idling, for girls, it was a space to cross quickly to reach home.

questions.text: The strike by Jaspreet in the story showed that the house ________.
options: Became a mess without her work
questions.explanation: It proved that her 'non-work' was actually essential for the family to function.

questions.text: Inequality between men and women is ________.
questions.explanation: The devaluation of women's work is a widespread issue found in many countries.

questions.text: Cleaning, washing, and sweeping are examples of ________ tasks.
questions.explanation: These are the core components of housework.

questions.text: Socialization is the process of ________.
options: Learning the norms and values of society
questions.explanation: It is how children learn what behavior is expected of them as boys or girls.

questions.text: Domestic workers often face ________ from their employers.
options: Lack of dignity and low pay
questions.explanation: Despite their hard work, they are often treated as inferior and paid very little.

questions.text: Cooking in a household involves standing for ________.
questions.explanation: It is a strenuous task that is often part of a woman's daily routine.

questions.text: The government acts to promote equality because ________.
questions.explanation: Upholding equality is a fundamental duty of a democratic government.

questions.text: Which of these is NOT a gender stereotype?
questions.explanation: Usually, the stereotype is that girls are *not* good at math; saying they are is less common as a traditional stereotype.

questions.text: In rural areas, women spend much time ________.
questions.explanation: These essential survival tasks are time-consuming and physically draining.

questions.text: The phrase 'Natural to women' is often used to ________ housework.
questions.explanation: By calling it 'natural', society avoids recognizing it as skilled labor that deserves value.

questions.text: A girl's identity is often shaped by her family's ________.
questions.explanation: Families play a key role in teaching children what it means to be a man or a woman.
